content:
Hi all,
for a university project, we are supposed to implement a kernel module which measures energy consumption estimates based on perf counters. We wanted to see if we could do this assignment in rust and potentially upstream some of our work. We don't have prior experience with interacting with the kernel community and processes, so I'm not sure if this is a sensible idea. From what I understood so far, I would need to reach out to the maintainer to see if they would even be interested in having rust bindings?

Welcome!

Yeah, that is correct. If you didn't see it yet, please take a look at https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submitting-new-abstractions-and-modules.

In general, I would strongly recommend not promising anything regarding upstreaming (especially if you have a deadline) unless you are sure the maintainers are OK with that.

However, you can still send it to the mailing list! For instance, you may consider sending your code as an RFC, even if maintainers do not want it -- I assume it would be an interesting experience for you, and a nice thing to say you did in your university project, and others may be able to benefit from that work.

I hope that helps.

https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submitting-new-abstractions-and-modules

Yeah, that's how I found the zulip chat :D
Regarding the upstreaming part, that wouldn't need to happen as part of the university project but I'm just interested in getting involved in the kernel community and this sounded like a good opportunity. I guess I'm a bit hesitant to ask for comment publically because I'm not well acquainted with the kernel way of doing things and am probably doing some stupid things. And I wouldn't want to invite arguments like "The code quality of these rust devs suck, they clearly don't know what they are doing" which is most likely correct in my case. I guess I'm basically wondering if there is a way to reach out for comment to code is in a somewhat presentable form before reaching out to the maintainers of the c code.
But I'll definitely try to write an RFC and clean up the code, this is all just very daunting.

because I'm not well acquainted with the kernel way of doing things and am probably doing some stupid things

Don't worry about any of that -- we all do stupid things. :)

People may point out mistakes in your code, but that is fine, and if it is just an RFC, it is not a big deal since it is not meant to be merged as-is anyway. It is more of a "hey, I have this code, do you think it is worth pursuing this?".

And I wouldn't want to invite arguments like "The code quality of these rust devs suck, they clearly don't know what they are doing" which is most likely correct in my case.

I appreciate that you thought of that in advance (that means you are wise), but I wouldn't worry. If you are really concerned, you can always mention that "this was done as my university project" and everyone should understand the context.

I guess I'm basically wondering if there is a way to reach out for comment to code is in a somewhat presentable form before reaching out to the maintainers of the c code.

Definitely, you can e.g. put a link to your branch here, or as a PR to our repository, and see what people say. We may not have time to do reviews, though, since we are already quite busy with what goes to the mailing list, but there is a lot of people watching the Zulip that may be able to help.

In general, my usual recommendation is that you try to make sure you understand how C equivalents of your module work, and document your code and decisions as best as you can. And any mistake may be a good time to update or improve the C docs/headers/etc. :)

By the way, most of this applies whether you do it in C or Rust.
Thanks for the kind words, I appreciate this warm welcome a lot. I'll try to do my best and let you go on with your (probably very busy) day

@Dennis Kobert
What h/w or arch are you looking to do this on? The performance counters are typically already consumed by 'perf' drivers and exposed by the perf core to userspace. If you are not using any of that, it is unlikely that a 2nd driver for the same h/w would be accepted upstream. If this is just a new perf driver in Rust, I'm not aware of any efforts on bindings. The perf subsystem is pretty unique compared to most kernel subsystems.

@Rob Herring

We are testing this with x86 intel cpus and are currently building abstractions for the perf_event_create_kernel_counter function and functions related to cpu power management. Our assignment is basically to write a kernel module to estimate the power consumption per process and to then enact policies to keep the system below a certain power target.
But yes, given that perf tools are available in the user space and that, even in the c code, the perf_event_create_kernel_counter is only used in a couple of places, this might not be particularly valuable

This is basically how using the abstraction currently looks like:

        let counter = EventBuilder::new(EventType::Hardware(HardwareEvent::CpuCycles))
            .cpu(CPU_NUM)
            .sample_period(SAMPLE_PERIOD)
            .on_overflow(|_, _, _| pr_info!("Perf counter has overflowed"))
            .disabled(); // Start disabled so we can control measurement
        counter.enable();
        counter.read();
        counter.disable();
